---
title: "Differential Predictive Value of Adiposity Indices for Glycemic Dysregulation Across the Menopausal Transition in Hausa Women"
author: 'A.Y Asuku^1^, M. A. Bashir^1,\*^ , J Muhammed^1^' 
output:
  bookdown::pdf_document2:
    latex_engine: xelatex
    number_sections: false
    reference_docx: jas_template.docx
bibliography: "`r rbbt::bbt_write_bib('mybibfile.bib', overwrite = TRUE)`"
csl: american-medical-association.csl
date: '`r Sys.Date()`'
---

^1^Department of Anatomy, Bayero University Kano, Kano State, Nigeria      
^2^Department of Anatomy, Bayero University Kano, Kano State, Nigeria 
\*Author of Correspondence, Email: [musabashir34\@gmail.com](mailto:musabashir34@gmail.com){.email}, GSM: +2347036878467

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE)
packages =c('tidyverse', 'tidymodels', 'ranger',
               'vip','kernlab',
               "glmnet","flextable", "corrplot", "patchwork")
lapply(packages, library, character.only = TRUE)

# source the script which writes the figures files to be read by the rmarkdown file

source("Analysis_script.R", local = knitr::knit_global())

#============================================================
# A. Table 1 formatting
#============================================================

# 1. Load the CSV data
table_1_data <- read.csv("figures_paperA/table-1.csv")

# Clean up column names automatically to handle hidden spaces
colnames(table_1_data) <- trimws(colnames(table_1_data))

# 2. Format columns to match the PDF Table 1 layout exactly
table_1_formatted <- table_1_data %>%
  mutate(
    # Clean up and expand the variable names with units
    Variable = case_when(
      Variable == "Age"         ~ "Age (years)",
      Variable == "BMI"         ~ "BMI",
      Variable == "Neck Circ."  ~ "Neck Circumference (cm)",
      Variable == "Hip Circ."   ~ "Hip Circumference (cm)",
      Variable == "Waist Circ." ~ "Waist Circumference (cm)",
      Variable == "WHR"         ~ "Waist-to-Hip-Ratio",
      Variable == "WHtR"        ~ "Waist-to-Height-Ratio",
      Variable == "BAI"         ~ "BAI (%)",
      TRUE                      ~ "HbA1c (%)"
    ),
    
    # Format P value dynamically (<0.001 for very small values, otherwise 3 decimals)
    'P_numeric' = as.numeric(sub("\\s*\\*+$", "", p_value)),
    `P value` = ifelse(p_value < 0.001, "<0.001", sprintf("%.3f", P_numeric))
  ) %>%
  # Select final columns in the correct layout order
  select(
    Variable,
    Premenopausal,
    Postmenopausal,
    `P value`
  )

#============================================================
# B. Table 2 formatting
#============================================================

# 1. Load the CSV data

table_2_data <- read.csv("figures_paperA/table-2.csv")

# Clean up column names automatically to handle hidden spaces
colnames(table_2_data) <- trimws(colnames(table_2_data))

# Helper function to format p-values cleanly (<0.001 or 3 decimals)
format_p <- function(p) {
  ifelse(p < 0.001, "<0.001", sprintf("%.3f", p))
}

# 2. Filter, clean, and merge columns to match Table 2 perfectly
table_2_formatted <- table_2_data %>%
  # Filter out "Age" as it is not included in the final correlation table
  filter(!trimws(Variable) %in% c("Age", "Variable")) %>%
  mutate(
    # Clean up and expand the variable names
    Variable = case_when(
      Variable == "BMI"         ~ "BMI",
      Variable == "Neck Circ."  ~ "Neck Circumference",
      Variable == "Hip Circ."   ~ "Hip Circumference",
      Variable == "Waist Circ." ~ "Waist Circumference",
      Variable == "WHR"         ~ "WHR",
      Variable == "WHtR"        ~ "WHtR",
      Variable == "BAI"         ~ "BAI",
      TRUE                      ~ as.character(Variable)
    ),
    
    # Format columns combining r and p-values: r (P-value)
    `All (r, P)` = sprintf("%.3f (%s)", r_all, format_p(p_all)),
    `Premenopausal (r, P)` = sprintf("%.3f (%s)", r_pre, format_p(p_pre)),
    `Postmenopausal (r, P)` = sprintf("%.3f (%s)", r_post, format_p(p_post))
  ) %>%
  # Select final columns in the correct layout order
  select(
    Variable,
    `All (r, P)`,
    `Premenopausal (r, P)`,
    `Postmenopausal (r, P)`
  )

#============================================================
# C. Table 3 formatting
#============================================================

# 1. Load the CSV data

table_3_data <- read.csv("figures_paperA/table-3.csv")

# Clean up column names automatically to handle hidden spaces
colnames(table_3_data) <- trimws(colnames(table_3_data))
# Force the first column header name to be exactly "Model"
colnames(table_3_data)[1] <- "Model"

# 2. Filter out repeating headers and combine estimates with standard deviations
table_3_formatted <- table_3_data %>%
  # Filter out any repeating column header rows safely
  filter(!trimws(Model) %in% c("Model")) %>%
  mutate(
    # Format RMSE ± RMSE_sd
    RMSE = sprintf("%.3f ± %.3f", as.numeric(RMSE), as.numeric(RMSE_sd)),
    
    # Format MAE ± MAE_sd
    MAE = sprintf("%.3f ± %.3f", as.numeric(MAE), as.numeric(MAE_sd)),
    
    # Format R2 ± R2_sd
    `R²` = sprintf("%.3f ± %.3f", as.numeric(R2), as.numeric(R2_sd))
  ) %>%
  # Select only the formatted presentation columns
  select(
    Model,
    RMSE,
    MAE,
    `R²`
  )

#============================================================
# D. Table 4 formatting
#============================================================

# 1. Load the CSV data

table_4_data <- read.csv("figures_paperA/table-4.csv")

# Clean up column names automatically to handle hidden spaces
colnames(table_4_data) <- trimws(colnames(table_4_data))
# Force the first column header name to be exactly "Model"
colnames(table_4_data)[1] <- "Feature"
# 2. Format columns to match the Table 4 layout perfectly
table_4_formatted <- table_4_data %>%
  # Filter out any repeating column header rows safely
  filter(!trimws(Feature) %in% c("Feature")) %>%
  mutate(
    # Format LASSO Coefficient to 3 decimal places
    `LASSO Coefficient` = sprintf("%.3f", as.numeric(LASSO_Coefficient)),
    
    # Format Random Forest MDI Importance to 1 decimal place
    `Random Forest MDI Importance` = sprintf("%.1f", as.numeric(RF_MDI_Importance))
  ) %>%
  # Select and organize the final columns
  select(
    Feature,
    `LASSO Coefficient`,
    `Random Forest MDI Importance`
  )

#============================================================
# E. Table 5 formatting
#============================================================

# 1. Load the CSV data

table_5_data <- read.csv("figures_paperA/table-5.csv")

# Clean up column names automatically to handle hidden spaces
colnames(table_5_data) <- trimws(colnames(table_5_data))
# Force the first column header name to be exactly "Model"
colnames(table_5_data)[1] <- "Group"
# 2. Format columns to match the Table 5 layout perfectly
table_5_formatted <- table_5_data %>%
  # Filter out any repeating column header rows safely
  filter(!trimws(Group) %in% c("Group")) %>%
  mutate(
    # Format Sample Size (N) as an integer text string
    N = sprintf("%d", as.integer(n)),
    
    # Format RMSE ± RMSE_sd matching the table's decimal layouts dynamically:
    # Premenopausal uses 3 decimals; Postmenopausal rounds to 2 decimals.
    RMSE = case_when(
      trimws(Group) == "Premenopausal"  ~ sprintf("%.3f ± %.3f", as.numeric(RMSE), as.numeric(RMSE_sd)),
      trimws(Group) == "Postmenopausal" ~ sprintf("%.2f ± %.3f", as.numeric(RMSE), as.numeric(RMSE_sd)),
      TRUE                              ~ sprintf("%.2f ± %.2f", as.numeric(RMSE), as.numeric(RMSE_sd))
    ),
    
    # Format R2 ± R2_sd to 2 decimal places for the main value
    `R²` = sprintf("%.2f ± %.3f", as.numeric(R2), as.numeric(R2_sd)),
    
    # Clean up and expand the text labels inside your Top 3 Predictors list
    `Top 3 Predictors` = Top3 %>%
      str_replace_all("Hip Circ.", "Hip Circumference") %>%
      str_replace_all("Neck Circ.", "Neck Circumference")
  ) %>%
  # Select and organize the final table columns
  select(
    Group,
    N,
    RMSE,
    `R²`,
    `Top 3 Predictors`
  )


```

# Abstract {-}

Objectives 

The menopausal transition is associated with shifts in fat distribution and declining glycemic regulation, but which anthropometric indices best capture this shift — and whether a single index generalizes across reproductive stages — remains unclear in West African populations. This study aimed to determine which of seven adiposity indices most reliably tracks glycated hemoglobin (HbA1c) in premenopausal and postmenopausal Hausa women, and whether their relative predictive value differs before and after the menopausal transition.

Methods

Cross-sectional study of 249 Hausa women aged 35 and older in Kano, Nigeria (119 premenopausal, 130 postmenopausal), comparing seven anthropometric indices — Body Mass Index (BMI), Body Adiposity Index (BAI), Waist-to-Hip Ratio (WHR), Waist-to-Height Ratio (WHtR), and neck, waist, and hip circumferences — as predictors of continuous HbA1c using classical and machine learning regression frameworks.

Main outcome measures

Laboratory-measured HbA1c correlated against seven adiposity markers, with predictive performance compared across Linear Regression, LASSO, Random Forest, and Support Vector Regression.

Results

HbA1c was significantly higher in postmenopausal than premenopausal women (7.29 ± 1.33% vs. 5.58 ± 0.65%; p < 0.001). BAI showed the strongest association with HbA1c in the pooled sample (r = 0.647) and was the only index to retain a significant correlation within both subgroups separately (premenopausal r = 0.450; postmenopausal r = 0.452), whereas the widely used WHR was not significant in either subgroup alone (pre p = 0.379; post p = 0.585), indicating its pooled correlation was driven by between-group differences rather than a within-person physiological relationship. In pooled models, Random Forest achieved the best fit (R² = 0.538 ± 0.022), narrowly ahead of LASSO (R² = 0.535 ± 0.034); LASSO regularization retained only BAI, menopausal status, neck circumference, and BMI as non-redundant predictors. When models were fit separately within each reproductive stage, predictive power was modest (premenopausal R² = 0.20 ± 0.085; postmenopausal R² = 0.17 ± 0.049), with BAI ranking as the top predictor in both, and neck circumference emerging as the second most important feature specifically in postmenopausal women.

Conclusions

As central adiposity becomes prevalent and uniform across an aging population, waist-based indices lose the variance needed to track glycemic change, while BAI — a total-adiposity, scale-free index — remains the more consistent physiological correlate across the menopausal transition. Neck circumference contributes independent predictive information, particularly after menopause, suggesting upper-body subcutaneous fat operates as a distinct driver of age-related dysglycemia. These findings point to BAI and neck circumference as promising, low-cost markers of metabolic aging in West African women, though anthropometry alone explains only a modest share of within-group variance and should be interpreted as one component of a broader assessment of glycemic risk.

Keywords

Reproductive aging; Body Adiposity Index; Glycated hemoglobin; Anthropometry; Neck circumference; West Africa

# Introduction

Reproductive aging is accompanied by well-documented shifts in body composition and glucose metabolism, yet the anthropometric indices used to track these shifts remain a matter of ongoing debate particularly in African populations, where patterns of fat distribution and its metabolic consequences differ from those described in European and Asian reference studies [@sekgala2024clinical;@Bergman2012]. As women transition from pre- to postmenopausal status, declining estrogen is thought to drive both a redistribution of adiposity and a decline in glycemic regulation, but it remains unclear which anthropometric markers most faithfully capture this physiological trajectory. Body Mass Index (BMI), the most widely used adiposity measure in clinical practice, has repeatedly shown weak or inconsistent correlation with glycemic biomarkers in African women, prompting interest in alternative indices better suited to tracking age-related metabolic change [@sekgala2024clinical; @freedman2012body]. To capture distinct localized fat depots, research has bifurcated into tracking central abdominal fat via Waist-to-Hip Ratio (WHR) and Waist-to-Height Ratio (WHtR), total weight-independent mass via the Body Adiposity Index (BAI), and upper-body subcutaneous fat via Neck Circumference (NC) [@Bergman2012]. Global consensus metrics heavily privilege waist-centric parameters for type 2 diabetes screening, while arguing that BAI's reliance on hip circumference renders it insensitive to viscerally driven metabolic risk [@freedman2012body; @chang2015evaluation]. However, these assumptions have rarely been tested against continuous long-term glycemic markers across the menopausal transition in West African populations.

Mechanistically, declining estrogen during and after menopause promotes a shift in fat distribution from gluteofemoral (peripheral) to abdominal (central) depots, accompanied by increased visceral adiposity, reduced insulin sensitivity, and increased hepatic glucose output [@lovejoy2008increased;@varlamov2015sex]. Glycated hemoglobin (HbA1c), which reflects average glycemia over the preceding two to three months and does not require fasting, offers a stable outcome for examining these longer-term associations with body composition [@ada2026diagnosis]. Several studies have reported that postmenopausal women exhibit a different pattern of association between adiposity indices and metabolic markers compared to premenopausal women [@juppi2022total], suggesting that a single universal index–outcome relationship may not generalize across reproductive stages. However, few studies have directly compared multiple adiposity indices' predictive performance for HbA1c separately within pre- and postmenopausal subgroups of the same population.

Hausa women in Kano, northern Nigeria, are an understudied population in this literature, despite representing one of West Africa's largest ethnic groups and exhibiting a high prevalence of central adiposity [@bashir2022prevalence]. Crucially, when central adiposity becomes highly prevalent and uniform across a specific demographic, traditional waist-centric metrics may suffer from a statistical ceiling effect, losing the predictive variance necessary to track linear changes in metabolic markers [@bashir2022prevalence]. In such settings, indices that capture total body volume or alternative fat depots, such as BAI and neck circumference, may offer superior predictive power [@article]. 

Because these adiposity indices are highly correlated with one another — sharing overlapping anthropometric components such as waist and hip circumference — conventional multivariable regression risks unstable or misleading coefficient estimates in the presence of this collinearity. A regression-focused comparison, spanning both regularized linear methods and non-linear ensemble approaches, allows redundant predictors to be identified and removed while also testing whether the underlying adiposity–HbA1c relationship is genuinely non-linear or is adequately captured by simpler, more clinically transparent models. This study aimed to quantify and compare the relationship between seven anthropometric adiposity indices and HbA1c, separately for premenopausal and postmenopausal Hausa women, and to determine whether the relative importance of indices differs between premenopausal and postmenopausal women.


# Methods

## Study design, setting, and population
This is a cross-sectional analysis of data collected from Murtala Muhammad Teaching Hospital (MMSHZ//GEN/0831/I) and Muhammad Abdullahi Wase Teaching Hospital (MAWTH/HMB/GEN/456/I), Kano, Nigeria. Ethical approval was obtained from both institutions and the Kano State Ministry of Health(NHREC/17/03/2018); all participants provided written informed consent. Adult Hausa women aged 35 years and above were enrolled and classified as premenopausal or postmenopausal (≥12 consecutive months of amenorrhea not attributable to a pathological cause). A recruitment floor of 35 establishes a robust, biologically stable pre-menopausal control group. Women in the 35–45 age bracket provide a clinically relevant point of comparison; they are close enough in chronological age to the post-menopausal cohort to minimize vast generational, lifestyle, and baseline metabolic discrepancies, yet they retain the standard gynoid fat distribution typical of active ovarian function. Chronological age was treated as a continuous covariate in our multivariable regression models, effectively isolating the impact of hormonal and anthropometric shifts from the standard aging process. Women with a prior diabetes diagnosis, current hormone replacement therapy use, pregnancy, or haematological conditions affecting HbA1c measurement were excluded. The final analytic sample comprised 249 women (119 premenopausal, 130 postmenopausal) with complete data.

## Measurements
Anthropometric measures included weight, height, BMI, waist circumference (WC), hip circumference (HC), neck circumference (NC), WHR, WHtR, and BAI. HC was measured at the level of the widest circumference over the great trochanters (WHO, 2008). WC was measured halfway between the lower ribs and the iliac crest. NC was measured with head in the Frankfurt horizontal plane and tape just below the thyroid cartilage [@isak2019standards]. BAI was calculated as:
$$BAI = \frac{HC \text{ (cm)}}{Height \text{ (m)}^{1.5}} - 18$$ [@Bergman2012]   
Serum HbA1c (%) was measured by ion-exchange high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC).

## Statistical and regression analysis
Descriptive statistics (mean ± SD) were computed for the full sample and stratified by menopausal status; group differences were tested using independent-samples t-tests. Pearson correlation coefficients between each adiposity index and HbA1c were computed for the full sample and separately within each menopausal subgroup.
Four regression approaches were compared:

• Linear Regression, as the classical baseline.
• LASSO Regression, which penalizes the sum of absolute coefficients, shrinking weak predictors to exactly zero and thereby performing automatic variable selection in the presence of collinear anthropometric indices [@tibshirani1996]. The regularization parameter (\lambda) was selected via internal cross-validation.
• Random Forest Regression, an ensemble of decision trees that does not assume linearity and provides a Mean Decrease in Impurity (MDI) importance score for each predictor [@breiman2001random].
• Support Vector Regression (SVR) with a radial basis function kernel, included as a further non-linear comparator [@cortes1995support].
All continuous predictors were standardized (zero mean, unit variance) prior to fitting LASSO and SVR, since these algorithms are scale-sensitive; Linear Regression and Random Forest were fit on unstandardized data, consistent with their scale invariance properties.
Model performance was assessed using 5-fold cross-validation, reporting Root Mean Square Error (RMSE), Mean Absolute Error (MAE), and the coefficient of determination (R²) averaged across folds. Subgroup-specific Random Forest models were also fit separately within premenopausal and postmenopausal women to assess whether the relative ranking of predictors changed by reproductive stage.
All analyses and data visualizations were conducted in R version 4.5.2 using tidyverse version 2.0.0 [@R-tidyverse], tidymodels version 1.5.0 [@R-tidymodels], ranger versioin 0.18.0 [@R-ranger], version 0.4.6 [@R-vip], kernlab version 0.9.33 [@R-kernlab], glmnet version 5.0 [@R-glmnet], flextable version 0.9.11 [@R-flextable], corrplot version 0.95 [@R-corrplot] and patchwork version 1.3.2 [@R-patchwork] packages.

# Results

Postmenopausal women (n=130) were significantly older than premenopausal women (n=119) (62.25 ± 7.05 vs. 41.07 ± 4.63 years; p < 0.001) and had significantly higher BMI, neck circumference, hip circumference, waist circumference, WHR, WHtR, and BAI (all p < 0.05). Mean HbA1c was significantly higher among postmenopausal women (7.29 ± 1.33%) than premenopausal women (5.58 ± 0.65%; p < 0.001), a difference of 1.71 percentage points (Table \@ref(tab:descriptives-table)). Figure \@ref(fig:descriptives-fig) shows the box-plots of the variables in the pre- and post-menopausal subjects.

```{r descriptives-table, tab.cap="Descriptive characteristics by menopausal status (mean ± SD) ", echo= FALSE}
flextable(table_1_formatted) %>% 
  set_table_properties(layout = "autofit")

```

```{r descriptives-fig, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, out.width= '100%', fig.cap="Age, HbA1c and Anthropometric Parameters of Pre- and Post-menopausal Subjects"}
knitr::include_graphics("figures_paperA/Figure-1.png")
```

## Correlations between adiposity indices and HbA1c

BAI showed the strongest correlation with HbA1c in the full sample (r = 0.647, p < 0.001), followed by WHtR (r = 0.573), Hip Circumference (r = 0.485), and Waist Circumference (r = 0.483). WHR showed the weakest association (r = 0.166) (Figure \@ref(fig:correlations-fig)). However, as shown in Table \@ref(tab:correlations-table), BAI and WHtR were the only indices to retain a strong, statistically significant correlation with HbA1c within both menopausal subgroups (BAI: pre r = 0.450, post r = 0.452; WHtR: pre r = 0.406, post r = 0.362), whereas WHR was non-significant in both subgroups when analyzed separately (pre p = 0.379; post p = 0.585) despite reaching significance in the pooled sample indicating its pooled correlation was driven by between-group rather than within-group variation.Figure \@ref(fig:correlations-by-group-fig) shows the scatterplot of BAI with HbA1c by menopausal status. 

```{r correlations-fig, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, fig.cap="Correlations Between Adiposity Indices and HbA1c"}
knitr::include_graphics("figures_paperA/Figure-2.png")
```




```{r correlations-table, tab.cap="Overall and Per Group Correlations Between Adiposity Indices and HbA1c", echo= FALSE}
flextable(table_2_formatted) %>% 
  set_table_properties(layout = "autofit")

```
 
 
 
```{r correlations-by-group-fig, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, fig.cap="Scatter Plots of BAI and HbA1c per Group"}
knitr::include_graphics("figures_paperA/Figure-3.png")
``` 

## Regression model comparison

As shown in Table \@ref(tab:regression-results-table)and Figure \@ref(fig:regression-results-fig), the predictive models demonstrated an explicit hierarchy in performance, with the advanced regularized and ensemble methods systematically outperforming the baseline parametric approach. Random Forest achieved the highest overall predictive accuracy and data fit, yielding the lowest errors (RMSE: \(0.960 \pm 0.047\); MAE: \(0.714 \pm 0.025\)) and explaining the largest proportion of variance in HbA1c (\(R^2: 0.538 \pm 0.022\)).The regularized linear framework, LASSO, emerged as the second-best predictive model, achieving an RMSE of \(0.974 \pm 0.039\), an MAE of \(0.725 \pm 0.020\), and an \(R^{2}\) of \(0.535 \pm 0.034\). By utilizing an automated grid search to tune the \(L_{1}\) penalty parameter (\(\lambda \)), LASSO successfully compressed non-contributing variance from redundant variables. This regularization allowed it to significantly outperform the standard baseline Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) Linear Regression, which exhibited higher prediction errors (RMSE: \(0.985 \pm 0.040\); MAE: \(0.738 \pm 0.023\)) and lower variance explanation (\(R^2: 0.525 \pm 0.031\)).Conversely, Support Vector Regression with a Radial Basis Function kernel [SVR (RBF)] underperformed relative to the other architectures. While it maintained competitive error metrics (MAE: \(0.719 \pm 0.030\)), it demonstrated the lowest and most volatile data fit (\(R^2: 0.500 \pm 0.022\)), accompanied by a higher error dispersion (RMSE: \(0.986 \pm 0.161\)).

```{r regression-results-table, tab.cap="Regression model performance (5-fold cross-validation)", echo= FALSE}

flextable(table_3_formatted) %>% 
  set_table_properties(layout = "autofit")

```





```{r regression-results-fig, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, fig.cap="Regression Mean Squared Errors and R-squared of the Models"}
knitr::include_graphics("figures_paperA/Figure-4.png")
```

## Variable selection and importance
LASSO regularization retained only four non-zero predictors: BAI (β = 0.493), menopausal status (β = 0.479), neck circumference (β = 0.155) and BMI (β = 0.0.03) (Table \@ref(tab:variable-selection-table)). All other indices (WHR, WHtR, waist circumference, hip circumference, and age) were shrunk to exactly zero, indicating their correlation with HbA1c was either redundant with BAI/neck circumference or too weak to survive regularization once collinearity was accounted for. Random Forest importance broadly corroborated this ranking: BAI carried the highest importance (MDI = 103.3), followed by menopausal status (73.1), with all remaining indices below 70. Figure \@ref(fig:variable-selection-fig) shows the variable selection and feature importance rankings for LASSO regression and Random Forest respectively.

```{r variable-selection-table, tab.cap="LASSO coefficients and Random Forest importance", echo= FALSE}

flextable(table_4_formatted) %>% 
  set_table_properties(layout = "autofit")

```




```{r variable-selection-fig, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, fig.cap="Variable Selection and Feature Importance of the Measures of LASSO regression (left) and Random Forest (right)"}
knitr::include_graphics("figures_paperA/Figure-5.png")
```

## Subgroup-specific predictor rankings
When Random Forest models were trained separately within each menopausal subgroup (excluding menopausal status as a predictor), BAI, WHtR and  HC were the three top predictors among premenopausal subjects while neck circumference ranked second pushing WHtR to the third position among the postmenopausal group (Table \@ref(tab:variable-selection-subgroup-table)).  Model fit was modest in both subgroups (premenopausal R² = 0.2 ± 0.085; postmenopausal R² = 0.17 ± 0.049), reflecting the narrower within-group HbA1c range once the dominant between-group menopausal effect is removed.

```{r variable-selection-subgroup-table, tab.cap="Random Forest Model by Menopausal Status", echo= FALSE}

flextable(table_5_formatted)%>% 
  set_table_properties(layout = "autofit")

```

# Discussion

## BAI as the most robust predictor
The consistent primacy of BAI as the strongest bivariate correlation, the largest LASSO coefficient, and the highest Random Forest importance, replicated within both menopausal subgroups provides convergent, multi-method evidence that BAI is the single most informative anthropometric index for HbA1c in this population. These findings contrast sharply with a large population based study among Chinese population consisting of 2419 males and 5140 females which found BAI to have the smallest correlation coefficient compared to central adiposity measures such as WHtR [@chang2015evaluation]. Similarly, another study on 10,663 Iranian cohorts reported WHR to have higher predictive accuracy than BAI [@ramezani2026novel]. Similar findings were reported among 2623 adult females in South Africa [@sekgala2024clinical]. The disparity of our findings from what was reported from these studies might be related to the fact that our population (Nigerian women) have a high prevalence of central adiposity [@bashir2022prevalence]. That this may be gender-specific finding is supported by the fact a study by Asuku et al [@yusuf2023predictability] in the same but gender-mixed population reveals WHR to be a better predictor of dysglycemia. If abdominal fat is highly prevalent and uniform across the cohort, waist measurements lose their variance (predictive power) and hip-and-height-based metrics like BAI might capture total metabolic tissue volume more accurately in this specific population. Because BAI is derived solely from hip circumference and height, without requiring a weighing scale, it may offer practical measurement advantages in field settings where reliable weighing scales are scarce. However, the modest within-group variance explained (Section 3.4) indicates it should complement, not replace, broader clinical assessment.

## The underperformance of WHR
The weak and non-significant within-group correlation of WHR with HbA1c contrasts with its frequent use as a default central-adiposity index in African metabolic research [@onuoha2016correlation;@peer2016differential]. One plausible explanation is that WHR's pooled correlation in this dataset is confounded by the large between-group age and menopausal differences rather than reflecting a genuine within-person physiological relationship. This is a pattern also seen for raw age in this analysis. This illustrates a broader methodological caution: pooled correlations across heterogeneous subgroups can overstate predictor utility unless subgroup-specific associations are also examined.

## Neck circumference as an anthropometric proxy for glycemic risk
A particularly compelling finding of this study is the resilience of neck circumference (NC) as a predictor of glycated hemoglobin (HbA1c). During feature selection via LASSO regularization, while highly praised central obesity metrics such as waist-to-hip ratio (WHR) and waist-to-height ratio (WHtR) were shrunk to zero due to multicollinearity, NC survived regularization and retained a positive predictive coefficient alongside the Body Adiposity Index (BAI). Sub-group analysis using Random Forest also revealed NC displacing WHtR as the second most important feature in predicing HbA1c levels. This outcome underscores that upper-body subcutaneous fat deposition exerts a distinct, independent influence on glycemic control that is not fully captured by traditional abdominal metrics [@li2023association;@pandey2024study;@zuo2026association].
Therefore, our findings suggest that a combined total-adiposity and upper-body subcutaneous marker (BAI and NC) warrants further evaluation as a minimally invasive screening approach for glycemic risk, pending validation of a specific combined scoring formula in future work.

## Linear relationships adequately explains HbA1c levels
The narrow performance gap between LASSO, Linear Regression SVR and Random Forest suggests the dominant adiposity–HbA1c relationships in this population are approximately linear. This is a clinically convenient finding: it supports the development of simple, transparent scoring formulas (e.g., a weighted sum of standardized BAI and a menopausal status indicator) rather than opaque algorithmic models, which is particularly relevant for resource-limited clinical settings where interpretability and ease of manual calculation matter. We have not yet derived or externally validated such a formula in the present sample; doing so, and testing it prospectively, is a natural next step given the approximately linear structure identified here.

## Menopausal status as a parallel, independent predictor
Menopausal status entered the LASSO model with a coefficient nearly equal in magnitude to BAI, indicating it contributes predictive information not captured by the adiposity indices themselves. This suggests that menopause's effect on HbA1c operates partly through pathways beyond simple fat redistribution, which are possibly direct effects of estrogen decline on hepatic glucose output and insulin sensitivity [@varlamov2015sex], and should be retained as an explicit covariate in any predictive model built on this or similar populations, rather than assumed to be fully mediated by adiposity measures.

## The post-menopausal gluteofemoral shift and the BAI paradox
Our study demonstrated a novel finding: BAI emerged as a superior predictor of HbA1c levels compared to traditional central obesity metrics in post-menopausal women. At first glance, this presents a physiological paradox. The post-menopausal transition is characterized by a loss of protective gluteofemoral subcutaneous fat [@manolopoulos2010gluteofemoral;@szeliga2026impact], an anatomical change that inherently reduces hip circumference and mathematically lowers individual BAI scores. So the higher metabolic risk might likely be associated with lower BAI scores contrary to the findings of this study. To understand why BAI outperformed central measures, the confounding effects of menopause on abdominal fat distribution must be considered. Following the withdrawal of estrogen, the shift toward visceral adiposity is a nearly universal phenomenon in aging women. Consequently, waist circumference may experience a 'saturation effect' within a post-menopausal cohort. Because central fat accumulation increases across the board, the statistical variance of waist metrics narrows, thereby reducing their discriminatory power to predict metabolic dysfunction like elevated HbA1c. 

In contrast, BAI, which relies on hip circumference, effectively serves as a proxy for global, lifetime adiposity in this demographic. While the post-menopausal transition reduces gluteofemoral fat relative to a woman’s youth, the absolute value of hip circumference remains highly variable across a population. A persistently high BAI in a post-menopausal woman indicates systemic fat overload that has outpaced the hormonal shift. In these individuals, the body's 'safe' subcutaneous metabolic sinks are entirely overwhelmed, triggering severe insulin resistance and a corresponding rise in HbA1c. Thus, the cross-sectional correlation remains highly robust.

Ultimately, our findings suggest that while central fat accumulation is a hallmark of menopause, relying solely on waist-based metrics may obscure individual metabolic risk due to population-wide shifts. By capturing global adiposity through hip dimensions, BAI provides a less statistically noisy signal, making it a highly sensitive tool for identifying advanced glycaemic risk and type 2 diabetes vulnerability in post-menopausal women.

## Limitations
This study is cross-sectional, hence causal inference is not possible, and unmeasured confounders (diet, physical activity, genetic factors) could not be assessed. The sample, while adequate for the primary regression comparisons, limited the precision of subgroup-specific models. Because premenopausal and postmenopausal women were compared as separate cross-sectional groups rather than followed longitudinally through the transition itself, the observed differences reflect a between-group snapshot rather than a within-person aging trajectory; longitudinal follow-up would be needed to confirm that these associations track the menopausal transition as it unfolds within individuals, rather than reflecting cohort differences between women who happened to be recruited at different reproductive stages."

# Conclusion

This study provides convergent evidence across correlation analysis, LASSO regularization, and Random Forest importance that BAI is the most consistent anthropometric correlate of HbA1c in Hausa women across the menopausal transition, outperforming the more conventionally used BMI and WHR in this population. Neck circumference contributes independent predictive value, particularly among postmenopausal women, pointing to upper-body subcutaneous fat as a distinct driver of age-related dysglycemia. Menopausal status itself contributes predictive value beyond its association with adiposity redistribution, suggesting that reproductive aging affects glycemic regulation through pathways not fully captured by anthropometry alone. However, predictive performance within each reproductive-stage subgroup remained modest (R² = 0.17–0.20), indicating that while BAI and neck circumference are promising low-cost markers for resource-limited settings, they should be regarded as one component of a broader assessment of glycemic risk in aging populations rather than a standalone diagnostic tool.

# Declaration of generative AI and AI-assisted technologies in the manuscript preparation process
During the preparation of this work the authors used Claude AI in order to map out the strategy for statistical analyses and validate the R code output using Python code. The generated Python code was tested and, when necessary, errors corrected. The authors take full responsibility for the analyses used in the published a

# Data availiability

The data and the R code for this analysis are available in the github repository https://github.com/musabashir34/Body_Adiposity_Index_Nigerian_Women

# References {-}
